narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 59% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic paths, family backgrounds, and bar outcomes, aiming to identify those likely to succeed right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. This stood out as the most influential concern, as stronger end-of-degree rankings often correlate with bar success in the model's view of historical trends. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA pushed strongly in the opposite direction, toward a passing prediction, since it's higher than most applicants and reflects consistent academic strength early on. Your LSAT score of 27, however, which is notably lower than the average of 36.7, added some weight toward the failure side, though less than your Year 3 decile. Your middle family income bracket and full-time student status aligned with the vast majority of applicants (over 92%), so they didn't sway things much either way. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows a promising start with that above-average GPA, but the dip in later school rankings and lower LSAT created a risk profile the model couldn't overlook. While your background as a white male doesn't directly factor into these key drivers, it fits within common patterns where academic metrics like these dominate bar predictions. This isn't a judgment on your potential—models like this are just one data-driven lens universities use to manage risks. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, strengthening your later academic showings through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience. Many students in similar spots reapply successfully after addressing these gaps, so use this feedback to refine your path.
